content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies That Take the Most Viewings to Truly Understand

**Score:** 64
**Body:** Which movies demand the highest number of viewings before they fully make sense? I’m not talking about films with just one big twist, but movies so layered, abstract, or complex that each rewatch is almost required to piece everything together.


Top voted comments win.


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Primer (2004) - include a rewatch with the director commentary so you can be mad too that there's unavailable deleted scenes

Edit: since everyone is getting lit up about my deleted scenes comment, they're not what you think 

If my memory serves me, Carruth said there's only 2 amounting to maybe 2 more minutes of runtime. One explains that Abe is diabetic and that's why he's meticulous, which I think should have stayed in because it would've showed a subtle character arc. Carruth explains he cut it because he wanted Aaron and Abe to appear more similar, albeit Aaron is married. Cutting it does emphasize Abe's subplot with his girlfriend because he's jealous of what Aaron has, but cutting it also weakens Abe's decline as he makes more and more mistakes in and to the timeline

I can't remember what the other one was about but Currath also mentions unrealized ideas about Thomas Ganger's time travel antics and how Abe is responsible for showing Thomas the time machine after telling Thomas about the circumstances of his daughter's death in one of the timelines

Edit #2: and before anyone gets on me for spoilers, you can't spoil this film. It's too confusing

Edit #3: some "weak sauce" challengers in the comments. "Remember the audience is "Grade 8"" rings true, I guess
2. I’ll add Upstream Color, same director (Shane Carruth) as Primer. Definitely an “interpretation film”, the kind of movie where, even after multiple viewings, your own interpretation might change.
3. memento, mulholland drive
  - Memento makes sense after one viewing. It's just even more fun once you've seen it.  Mulholland drive, still doesn't make complete sense to me.
    - it probably didn't make complete sense to David Lynch either
4. Primer
5. Granted I was 15 but I had to watch The Big Lebowski like 3 times before I knew who the fukk the Knudsens were.
6. to me it was Synecdoche, New York (2008), maybe I'm a bit dumb but it took me a while to get it, great movie though
7. Akira.
8. It took me a rewatch to understand and appreciate Midsommar. I had to understand the meaning of the filmmaker to fully understand - its about breakup / letting go.

And everytime I rewatch Interstellar I understand better!

## 2. Looking for a movie like Three Days of the Condor with Robert Redford

**Score:** 36
**Body:** I recently rewatched *Three Days of the Condor* and really loved the tense, smart thriller vibe and Redford's performance in it. I'm hoping to find other films, maybe from the same era or style, that have a similar feeling of suspense and strong character-driven storytelling.

Does anyone have suggestions for movies, especially ones starring Robert Redford or similar actors, that capture that kind of mood and pacing? I'd appreciate any recommendations. Thanks.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. All the President's Men (1976), The Parallax View (1974), The Day of the Jackal (1973), Marathon Man (1976).
  - I love 70's political thrillers
2. Great movie, one of my favorites!

The Taking of Pelham 1-2-3, the original from the same era is just as good.
3. The conversation
  - Excellent film. The final scene is chilling!
4. Marathon Man with Dustin Hoffman, you'll never want to go to the dentist again.
  - This is the first one that came to mind for me too. Very similar feel.
5. Spy Game (2001) also starred Redford. It was fantastic.
6. The Odessa File

Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy... the 1979 BBC adaptation starring Sir Alec Guinness.  IMO, much better and truer to the book than the later remake.

Torn Curtain
7. A Most Wanted Man

Sneakers

No Way Out

The Old Man & The Gun

## 3. horror comedies with brutal deaths

**Score:** 29
**Body:** im looking for what the title say ,ive been on the hunt for more horror comedies but with brutal and gorey movies . my favorite horror comedy is Tucker and Dale vs evil ,actually the best ive seen ,BUT I WANT MOREEEEE ,please help!!!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cabin in the Woods. Not exactly a "comedy" per se, but it does satirize a lot of horror tropes and is genuinely a fun movie.
2. Idle Hands (1999)
3. American Werewolf in London
4. The Monkey
5. Ready or Not
6. Braindead. Peter Jackson's second movie. It's hilarious and extremely gory in the best possible way. I can't recommend this movie enough.
7. Zombieland 1 and 2
8. Shaun of the dead
9. Evil dead 3 army of darkness
10. Tucker and Dale vs Evil

## 4. What are some romantic movies that are themed around loneliness?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I’m looking for Wong Kar-wai type of movies. In the mood for love, chunking express, happy together. Specifically fallen angels, this is one of my favorite movies of all time. Lost in Translation and Her are also movies that fall into this tone and style that I also love. I also feel like blue valentine falls into this sub-genre of romantic dramas. I wanna feel really shitty with a glimmer of hope. Punch Drunk love is also one of my favorites that really falls into this category. I’m not looking for tragic romances, i’m looking for movies that are themed around loneliness and involve a character finding a person or space that brings some levity to aspects of their life or self that they are missing. 

I also love the cinematography of all these films so bonus points if the suggestion has a very dream like aesthetic to them.

Very specific ik but i’m out of luck finding suggestions that resemble this tone and theming. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eternal sunshine
2. Past Lives and Before Sunrise
3. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind


Monster (Kaibutsu) by Kore-eda
4. The apartment
5. Her
6. Midnight in Paris

Before Sunrise

All of Us Strangers

Call Me by Your Name

Passengers

The Lobster

The Perks of Being a Wallflower

Brokeback Mountain

Lars and the Real Girl
7. Paris, Texas

Past Lives 

Equals 

Wong kar-wai’s 2046 shares similar dna to mood for love.
8. A Single Man, Past Lives, Yearning, Dolls, Norwegian Wood, Take This Waltz, The Taste of Things, Crouching Tiger.

Here’s a list I made with other recs, some of them don’t match your criteria 100%, but still are a great exploration of love.  
[https://boxd.it/NmhK2](https://boxd.it/NmhK2)
9. Submarine
10. Somewhere in time (1980)

## 5. Movies to cheer me up

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Dog had to have surgery yesterday.  Feeling very down.  What are people's favorite comfort / happy / comedy movies?  

Some of my favorite comedies:  Bridesmaids, Wedding Crashers, Forgetting Sarah Marshall, I Love You, Man, Pineapple Express, Old School, You, Me, and Dupree, 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amelie
2. Big Fat Greek Wedding or My Life in Ruins
3. Role Models (2008) 

Superbad (2007) 
	
Step Brothers (2008) 

Tropic Thunder (2008) 

Horrible Bosses (2011)
4. Go for the light English comedy:  
The Full Monty  
Calendar Girls  
The Englishman Who Went Up A Hill and Came Down A Mountain  
Waking Ned Divine
5. The ultimate dog movie, BEST IN SHOW!  One quote by the late great Fred Willard “It looks like her dress was made out of ham.” And, “Yes, why he does have two left feet!”  And finally, “Do you have busy bee?”
6. Booksmart
7. The Peanut Butter Falcon 

Dinner in America

Rye Lane

Riders of Justice

Romantics Anonymous 

The Wedding Banquet

Tampopo
8. The great outdoors
9. I like Rat Race. Very funny
10. The Sting and Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. 20 years ago, V for Vendetta was giving us a glimpse of our future

**Score:** 1317
**Body:** Great movie for it's time, sends a deep message. Who would've thought the message would hit this deep today. So much for the first amendment

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I just rewatched in light of recent events. There is something very wrong with this country. We've lost our tolerance for each other. Our leaders would rather see us divided than united. Make no mistake about it, we're entering a new paradigm. I've never been so ashamed, fearful, and disgusted with this country that I truly love. I hope I'm wrong.
  - Its easier to line ones pockets when the marks are too busy pointing their fingers at each other to care to stop you.

Look at any financial records of someone standing in front of a mic,  telling you to hate someone.

I bet they are doing well, more often than not.
2. Watched it again a few weeks ago, very different feeling now than the first watch when it came out.
  - surveillance, censorship, oppression, and authoritarianism suddenly don't seem like some distant concepts that happen in far away lands.
3. See also, from around a similar time, Children Of Men.
  - Can't believe i haven't seen that one yet. Gonna check it out, thank u
    - It's a great film. Gives a view of Britain, closed borders, under a far far right dictatorship.
Incredible climax too, all one continuous shot.
4. It was such a bad adaptation of the book. The also really messed up the ending. It wasn't about everyone banding together and showing unity against the government, in the book it was about the need for total chaos to erupt and burn down society before something new (and hopefully better) can be built
  - Came to say this too.  read the book.  Still appreciate the movie
  - > burn down society before something new (and hopefully better) can be built

When has this ever happened?

## 2. Examples of twin movies?

**Score:** 747
**Body:** Olympus Has Fallen and White House. Two movies I consider to be “twins”. Both were released in 2013, each one follows either an ex-secret service or a rejected secret service member. After the White House is attacked, they take it into their own hands to save the day and take on the enemy whilst saving the president. They even have almost identical scores on Rotten Tomatoes and IMDb. 

Olympus Has Fallen
IMDb: 6.5/10 | Rotten Tomatoes: 50%

White House Down
IMDb: 6.3/10 | Rotten Tomatoes: 52%

What are some other examples of “twin movies”? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/two-movies-with-the-same-plot-released-in-the-same-year-with-almost-identical-ratings-from-critics-and-audiences

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Volcano and Dante’s Peak are also this.
2. No strings attached and friends with benefits
3. Antz and "A Bug's Life"
4. Armageddon and Deep Impact
5. I always considered the Illusionist (2006) and the Prestige (2006) as twins
  - twins you say.....
6. White House Down needed an R-rated cut.

Topic: Tombstone and Wyatt Earp.
7. The Truman Show (1998) and Ed TV (1999)
8. The Abyss, Leviathan,  and Deep Star Six all coming out in 1989
9. The Jungle Book (Jon Favreau) and Mowgli (Andy Serkis)

## 3. Poor Bruce

**Score:** 544

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Rachel: "Do better."  
Bruce: \*Does better\*  
Rachel: "NOT LIKE THAT!"
2. He should stick with the Russian ballet! Much less drama!
  - >You can tell me the Russian for, 'Apply your own bloody suntan lotion'
3. Rachel was the worst character in both movies.
  - RACHEL!!!!
4. Fine then I'll just go save your boyfriend, that guy I hate, instead of you
  - That was Bruce's revenge
5. What was her excuse in the Dark Knig... oh... yeah... 
  - "I can't love you 'cause I got blowed up."
6. Everyone in Bruce’s life through the trilogy recognizes that Bruce and Batman are inseparable.  That’s part of his growth in Rises

He’s finally able to let go of the cape and cowl.

## 4. Can you name a movie that wastes no time, hops right into the premise, and stays there more than Bedazzled?

**Score:** 386
**Body:** I’m not saying it’s the best movie of all time, and some things have not aged well, but you gotta respect the fact that it’s in the meat of it in like 15 minutes and stays there until 15 minutes from end.

It’s a movie about a guy making a bunch of cursed wishes with the devil while the devil chews up the scenery. And that’s what you get.

It doesn’t take half the movie to get there.

It doesn’t do a cheap montage.

It doesn’t do “saves the cat” bullshit.

It delivers all the way through to the very end.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You’ve just given me the last push I needed to watch this movie
  - Enjoy! The director's commentary is worth finding also.
2. Considering his recent passing. (Robert Redford) Spy Game.

*Bedazzled is excellent 👌😈
3. This is the movie that proved Brendan can act. He is excellent in this as every ridiculous character. Such a fun night in movie
4. You may need to refine your conditions. All I can think of are movies that do this: Star Wars, Jaws, Anchorman, Trainspotting, Saving Private Ryan, most Kevin Smith, Scorsese, and Tarantino movies…I don’t know…am I missing something?
5. Baby driver
6. Pulp fiction
  - I would argue that Pulp Fiction is almost the opposite of this. 

The first time I watched it I kept wondering where the heck we were even going here. It starts, stops, rewinds, and meanders in whatever direction it pleases.

But the beauty of it is you’ve been following the plot the whole time, and almost not even realizing it.
  - Damn jimmie this some gourmet shit
7. You just gotta give 110%

## 5. What do you think is the coolest movie weapon? My pick is the Mulcher gun from District 9

**Score:** 263

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Zorg ZF-1 from the fifth element
  - What about the red button
2. The Noisy Cricket
  - More of a Series 4 De-Atomizer guy myself
3. M41-A Pulse Rifle from Aliens
4. All the tech in this movie was unique and badass. I love Neill Blomkamp’s aesthetic vision.
5. Mega Maid
  - She’s gone from suck to blow.
6. I mean, the Death Star.
7. The custom silenced Remington 11-87 shotgun used by Javier Bardem’s character Anton Chigurh in No Country for Old Men

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. "It's very interesting playing someone who is incredibly powerful, all dominant, expects everyone to bow to their every need and thought and want, and yet is sitting...kind of in his own rotting flesh and having to face himself, and he can't escape what he's done to himself and who he's become..."

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Jude Law speaking on today's episode of Fresh Air, regarding his performance of Henry VIII in 2023's Firebrand. Perhaps this is more suited to 'Fun & Fancy Free' discussion (will maybe re-post there if deemed unsuitable for here) but, at the expense of being inflammatory, I can't be the only one who couldn't help but immediately draw a parallel with another divisive leader of our time. It's not a stretch. Hell, one could argue that this quote applies to everyone from Genghis Khan to Julius Caesar and beyond.

Every modern president has had films made about them, successful or otherwise: JFK, Game Change, Vice, Frost-Nixon, Southside With You, The Apprentice and so on. It's inevitable that there will be more to come, however the division that the current administration has fomented, along with the cast of characters involved, it is all so ripe for insidious conflict that I believe some time in the next 20 years we could be/will be treated to film work based on our time period that is really special. Imagine Sorkin and Fincher tackling the story of the last months (if there are any) of this administration, it could be utterly captivating.

The problem becomes, are people too tired of \*gestures to everything\* to see the art? How long before audiences want to see behind the curtain (how long before they're allowed?) Trump has been broadcast, caricatured and impersonated to an unprecedented extent for the past decade or so, that to many people it's just exhausting. For better or worse, he's such a fascinating, discordant, magnetic, repellant character that things will be a lot more dull once he's gone. 

At this stage of the narrative I can't help but think we're in for a film in the mold of *Downfall* rather than *W.,* but I'd love to hear your thoughts on which filmmakers you can foresee tackling the drama that is currently unfolding before our eyes.

Full quote: 

*"It's very interesting playing someone who is incredibly powerful, all dominant, expects everyone to bow to their every need and thought and want, and yet is sitting in a body that is immobile because of the weight he's put on and because of the wounds he has, kind of in his own rotting flesh and having to kind of face himself, and he can't escape what he's done to himself and who he's become, you know he's a mass murderer and deluded to the extreme of believing that he's second only to God. Well he's about to face God."*

**-Jude Law, Fresh Air, Sept 18 2025**

PS: I'm not saying there are exact parallels. That probably would require some capacity for self-reflection by DJT, but again that's what makes him such an interesting character.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. Twinless and its depiction of loneliness

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I went to see Twinless a week or so ago and thoroughly enjoyed the movie. While the general movie navigates us through the grief and evolution of Roman after his twin dies, there is an underlying theme of loneliness that is pretty well depicted. 

Twinless shows loneliness in various different ways and how differently is is/can be coped with. Dennis is almost the stereotypical lonely person. He has no real friends or connections. He doesn't really seem to *want* to connect with people, though we know that isn't true. When Marcie talks to old classmates, they corroborate that he was the kid that sat alone at lunch and barely talked. Dennis is also the one that goes to the most extreme to develop and keep connections. He was partially involved in Roman's twin (Rocky) death due to his inability to let connections that weren't there drop. This then tumbles into him having to build a lie to keep in touch with Roman that all comes crashing down at the end of the movie, leaving him right back at square one. 

Rocky (Roman's twin) represents another more common type of loneliness. Rocky is attractive, gay and easily hooks up with men. He is able to have company to stave off loneliness at a whim (thus how he met Dennis), but those are just distractions to keep the deep loneliness at bay. In a twisted sort of way, Dennis represented an out that Rocky had likely ignored time and time again-a person who liked him and would have been open to more than a hookup. 

It's a bit difficult to classify Roman's loneliness. He has a lot of commonalities to Dennis. Having a twin kept him from having to really try and reach out to anyone and make friends. When Rocky went away to college, then went abroad, Roman was left to his own devices and alone figuratively and literally. His brother evolved and changed and left him behind. The angst, bitterness and sadness pushed him further into solitude. 

Ironically, the person Dennis assumed to be the most socially awkward and lonely wasn't (Marcie). She had many friends who came to her party, had ways to reach out and find out who Dennis was, etc. 

Not sure how to end this or whether this was a useless rant, but the undertone of loneliness in Twinless worked well and I'm sure someone else will pull out more than I did.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I wouldn't really say it's underlying tbh, because so much of what drives each character's decision-making is isolation and desperation - but I think this movie has so many layers both thematically & comedically that make it so moving. Many people would say Dennis' loneliness is self-inflicted, but it doesn't necessarily demonize him for it, only condemns his actions with harsh consequences. I love this movie so much, I saw it in a film fest screening earlier this year and the entire theatre was raucous throughout in the best way. 

Sweeney is one of the most exciting writers out and his visual eye is seriously impressive for such a young director. 

I think what got people through the door was Dylan O'Brien's (well-deserved) raves for his performance but what sticks with you is how starkly alone everyone in this story is. Even Marcie is implicated as a victim of other people's hurt and suffers herself as a result.

## 3. The Chess game and the newscast - those are the key to understanding the Jupiter Mission segment of 2001

**Score:** 10
**Body:** **I have taken my comments on a earlier post and combined them together in a somewhat coherent form. I had thought about posting these ideas in the past but never got around to it.**  

Everyone agrees that there are no unintentional or accidental things in Kubrick movies, yet they ignore the lie that HAL told during the Chess game with Poole. Poole seemed clearly confused and overmatched in the game.  

I think that as a result of that, HAL tested Poole, and Poole failed the test. Basically HAL told Poole that the game was over:  

*Poole resigns the game once HAL indicates a certain path to checkmate; however, the move which HAL suggests Frank might make is not forced. Stanley Kubrick, director of 2001, was an avid chess player.*

 ref: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poole_versus_HAL_9000


The News interview:  

In this interview, the crew claims that they treat HAL like any other crewmember, but they don’t. They lie to him and treat him as a child that they are suspicious of. The moment something weird shows up in his behavior, they immediately and obviously start discussing disconnecting him. They would not immediately jump to that if he was just another crewmember. HAL was protecting himself from what he saw as defective and suddenly homicidal members of the mission.  

HAL was the protagonist of that segment of the movie. It is a tragedy (in the Shakespearean sense), with HAL losing his life in combat with other beings. Just like in the monkey combat scene. Then the winner goes on to their winnings/destiny. It could have been HAL that met the aliens, and then HAL would have ascended instead of Bowman. To the victor go the spoils.

> But HAL was programmed to take over if the humans failed, didn't he just follow his programming?

No.  

HAL *tried* to talk to Bowman. HAL made up an excuse to draw him in and show interest in his (poor) drawings (along with pretending that he needed them to be held up to his “eye” to show interest and to drum up a conversation). **HAL starts asking him questions about the mission because HAL is concerned and he is trying to have a real conversation.** Like you would with a fellow “crewman”.  

But Bowman senses an attack— checking loyalty or for weakness - and “defends” himself by suggesting that HAL is testing him. At this point in the movie, this is the only change in the speed at which HAL replies—it is almost imperceptibly longer before he replies to Bowman, then replying that it was a test.  

But HAL lied. He answered Bowman’s disingenuousness with his own. He learned to protect himself. Just like the apes. And very similar to the conversation in the space station where they were trying to get the real story from Floyd about the moon. Put HAL in the place of those concerned international scientists trying to get Floyd to talk, and how slickly Floyd handled them and deflecting their concerns and just not saying anything. This is exactly how Poole treated HAL in that conversation.    

Bowman was **never** just going to volunteer doubts to a machine that was literally ordered to monitor his performance and test him. This is an astronaut/pilot thing.  

Kubrick cast him for THAT face in that scene, that stupid faux concerned interested look which is Keir Dullea’s default look.  

The only thing that could have saved this situation would have been for HAL to admit to Bowman that it harbored doubts and wanted to talk about it. This would have been seen by Bowman as HAL risking itself, opening itself up. I think that it would have caused Bowman to see HAL as more than just a fancy machine.  

Then, before anyone can ask any further questions, the equipment malfunction is announced—a misdirection by HAL. It was a panic move perhaps. Maybe he did not expect them to react the way they did—because re-installing the original unit and it not failing is what made everything worse, and spiked his fellow crewmen’s suspicion levels, leading to the “secret” conversation in the pod. Which HAL, with his actually **excellent** vision, was able to read their lips.  

Obviously if HAL can read lips from 30+ feet away, through a porthole, *then he absolutely did not need to have Bowman bring the drawings closer to his “eye”.* That was HAL showing he already was able to tell a white lie, and showed it knew *when* to tell one.

Ironically, most likely the reason that they didn’t just take HAL’s word that the part was going to fail and simply replace it is *because* of that aborted conversation with Bowman. Bowman was already suspicious, so he decided to test HAL. When the part did not go bad, they assumed the worst—that HAL had gone crazy. They could have simply replaced the part with the spare and NOT examined the old one. If they had chosen that, HAL’s lie would never be revealed and there would have been no conflict.

> But wasn't HAL trying to cut off the astronauts from communication with Earth?  

HAL controlled every part of the ship. HAL could have made any part of it fail or simply take control. HAL had no interest in severing contact with Earth. It was interested in finishing the mission.  

HAL panicked when Bowman called him on questioning the mission and HAL wanted to change the subject. Just like a human might do. The antenna failure is the lie that it picked. I don’t think it was part of an overall scheme.  


Kubrick tells us (in the news interview) that HAL should be seen as just another crewman.  
**Try listening to the HAL conversations with the crew, but instead imagine HAL as a crewman instead of a disembodied voice with a glowing red eye. It will really change your perspective.**  

Kubrick made HAL look so different than a person to fool us into thinking of him as a robot, just like Bowman does. But read the exchanges as written. HAL is a crew member and behaves as one until Bowman and Poole turn on him after its lie.  

**If you were part of a three man crew, and you just watched the other two discuss killing you, you’d probably do something about it too.**

*Things like that news interview exposition are how Kubrick tells you what is really happening. He gives you the tools to understand, but not the actual message. And he does it so subtly, that even film experts do not see it.*  

Kubrick liked screwing with the critics. He wanted to impress them with his visuals, but he enjoyed putting a message out there that had an effect on the viewer that the critics themselves could not understand.  

Kubrick was a genius that will never be matched.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always got the impression that HAL wasn't being merely malevolent. The explanation that he wanted to preserve the mission at all costs, even sacrificing the crew for it always felt an inadequate explanation for everything that takes place. As you say, discussing killing your crew mate, hell, even the notion that the two other crew are definitely conspiring against you in some way is definite cause for alarm.

I see this malevolence assumed in many AI/Android characters when it can sometimes be rationally explained by treating them as sapient beings.

I'm definitely going to give 2001 another watch with this in mind, thanks for giving me an excuse!
2. Good write up. My theory: HAL tested the crew the same way he would test any other aspect of the ship. Basic diagnostics. He told them to replace the AE-35 unit and let it fail. (He would have made it fail to prove he was right.) They refused, so they had proven themselves faulty (not following orders) so they had to go to in order to save the mission.

## 4. Casual Discussion Thread (September 18, 2025)

**Score:** 4
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Weekend at Bernie's is a great movie.

## 5. I've just seen SENTIMENTAL VALUE and … (non-spoilery review)

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Okay, so—I got to see this film last night and I got very excited … so here's the fruity review I wrote on Letterboxd over breakfast today. And yes, the film does bear comparison to CLOUDS OF SILS MARIA, which has been a favourite of mine since its release, but I think it might be better.

Whether or not you read what's below I'd love to hear what you thought about it.

---
  
Wow. There are, at least, a jaw-droppingly good and pyroelectric script, surgical technique, and rich performances here. SENTIMENTAL VALUE is a sinewy beast in a familiar familial form, possessing so much folding and unfolding capacity that's been put to work doing all sorts of things.

SENTIMENTAL VALUE has brilliant actors acting brilliantly, actors brilliantly acting bad actors acting badly, characters with tormented relations characterised by the relentless frustration of each attempt to stitch them up or straighten them out, true love and the mystery of its origins, elaborate witticisms, extended and delightful metafictional fake-outs, nuanced recollections of luminous cinema history and bloody theatrical history and corrupt human history, magic tricks of technical filmmaking that are first announced to us and then confidently display their workings with dizzying transparency ... this story precipitates like Ibsen shattered through the crystalline mind's eye of Borges, demonstrating the refractive, kaleidoscopic excess of the ostensibly minimal realism of every quotidian humanity.

If there's a great deal going on here, few or no sacrifices of character or drama have been needful to contrive what is achieved. Because this drama—and it is insinuated, every drama—inheres in the gap between the truth established by what it performs and the elusions of its essence, its high concepts amplify its affects. While its finale can and does deliver all the much-desired feeling of an irrevocable transfer of the stakes the whole setup has laboured to develop, paying off every promise diligently, it emphasises the very manner of being of endings is a connective and ambiguous openness to further constructions.

All along, as it turns out, we have been touring a monument of generative self-awareness. SENTIMENTAL VALUE ends by plaintively expressing to us how the drama of its own creation has only been managed on the speculative foundation of a formally unresolved, and unresolvable relation to its future audiences.

Rather like any parent isolated in a needy or standoffish moment, creative team Joachim Trier and Eskil Vogt, and the superb cast led by Stellan Skarsgård and Renate Reinsve, hope to have taught us what it is to love in the further vulnerable hope of being loved in return. To call this manoeuvre relatable would be to understate its ambitions. SENTIMENTAL VALUE produces general intensities of human relation with such an assured anxiety each viewer could imagine sharing in some pure and collective ramification of private conscience, in which each other viewer also feels urged on to give tenderness to those whom they have.

Beneath or behind every fictional image that filmmakers, actors and audiences organise, construct, inhabit and renovate together, every old family home and closing door, must persist the unseen, contingent and traumatic knots and tangles of the stage machinery that permits its projection.
